---
title: "06_mitochondrial_score"
format: html
---

## mitochondrial score

```{python}
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
```

```{python}
#load MitoCarta gene list
mitocarta = pd.read_excel("Human.MitoCarta3.0.xls", sheet_name="A Human MitoCarta3.0")
```

```{python}
mitocarta.head()
```

```{python}
print(mitocarta.columns.tolist())
```

```{python}
mito_genes = set(mitocarta["Symbol"].dropna().unique())
len(mito_gene)
```

```{python}
data_filtered = pd.read_csv("data_filtered.csv")
data_filtered = data_filtered.set_index("gene_id")
data_filtered.head()
```

```{python}
genes_metadata = pd.read_csv("tcga_brca_gene_metadata.csv", index_col=0)
genes_metadata.head()
```

```{python}
# Map Ensembl IDs to gene symbols 
data_filtered["gene_symbol"] = data_filtered.index.map(genes_metadata["gene_name"])
data_filtered = data_filtered.dropna(subset=["gene_symbol"])
data_filtered = data_filtered.drop_duplicates(subset="gene_symbol", keep="first")
data_filtered = data_filtered.set_index("gene_symbol")
```

```{python}
#Log-transform (variance stabilization)
log_expr = np.log2(data_filtered +1)
```

```{python}

#Subset to MitoCarta genes actually present in data
mito_genes_present = [g for g in mito_genes if g in log_expr.index]
print(f"MitoCarta genes found in your dataset: {len(mito_genes_present)} "
      f"out of {len(mito_genes)}")
 
mito_expr = log_expr.loc[mito_genes_present]
```

# 

```{python}
# Compute a per-sample mitochondrial score
# Why z-score first, not just average raw expression: genes have very
# different baseline expression levels and variances. Without
# z-scoring, a handful of very highly-expressed genes would dominate
# the score regardless of whether they actually change between samples.
# Z-scoring puts every gene on the same scale (mean 0, SD 1) before
# combining them.

gene_means = mito_expr.mean(axis=1)
gene_stds = mito_expr.std(axis=1)
z_scores = mito_expr.sub(gene_means, axis=0).div(gene_stds, axis=0)
mito_score = z_scores.mean(axis=0)   # average across genes, per sample
mito_score.name = "mitochondrial_score"
 
print("\nMitochondrial score summary:")
print(mito_score.describe())
 
mito_score.to_csv("mitochondrial_score_per_sample.csv")

```

```{python}
#Compare score between tumor and normal
sample_meta = pd.read_csv("sample.csv", index_col=0)
sample_meta["mito_score"] = mito_score.reindex(sample_meta.index)
 
tumor_scores = sample_meta.loc[
    sample_meta["tcga.cgc_sample_sample_type"] == "Primary Tumor", "mito_score"
]
normal_scores = sample_meta.loc[
    sample_meta["tcga.cgc_sample_sample_type"] == "Solid Tissue Normal", "mito_score"
]
 
print(f"\nMean mitochondrial score, tumor:  {tumor_scores.mean():.3f}")
print(f"Mean mitochondrial score, normal: {normal_scores.mean():.3f}")
 
from scipy.stats import mannwhitneyu
stat, pval = mannwhitneyu(tumor_scores.dropna(), normal_scores.dropna())
print(f"Mann-Whitney U test p-value: {pval:.2e}")
```

tumor samples show a slightly higher average mitochondrial score (0.010) than normal samples (-0.099), not lower. Tumors often show increased mitochondrial biogenesis and altered mitochondrial dynamics even while specific energy-production pathways (OXPHOS, fatty acid oxidation) go down. So "more mitochondrial genes overall" and "less oxidative energy production specifically" aren't actually contradictory, they can both be true if the mitochondrial changes are about remodeling, not just shutting down.

## MitoCarta3.0 Pathway-level sub-scores

Instead of one score across all 1,058 mitochondrial genes, break it down by MitoCarta's own curated functional pathway categories (OXPHOS, mtDNA maintenance, protein import, etc.) using the "MitoPathways" sheet.

This lets us test directly whether the overall mitochondrial score going UP in cancer, while OXPHOS/fatty-acid genes go DOWN in GSEA, is because different mitochondrial sub-functions are moving in different directions at the same time.

```{python}
mitopathways = pd.read_excel(
    "Human.MitoCarta3.0.xls",
    sheet_name="C MitoPathways"
)
 
print(mitopathways.columns.tolist())
print(mitopathways.head())
```

```{python}
GENES_COL = "Genes"
PATHWAY_COL = "MitoPathway"
HIERARCHY_COL = "MitoPathways Hierarchy"
```

```{python}
#Parse each pathway's gene list into a proper Python list
def parse_genes(gene_string):
    if pd.isna(gene_string):
        return []
    # MitoCarta typically separates with spaces; adjust to "," if needed
    return [g.strip() for g in str(gene_string).replace(",", " ").split() if g.strip()]
 
mitopathways["gene_list"] = mitopathways[GENES_COL].apply(parse_genes)
mitopathways["n_genes"] = mitopathways["gene_list"].apply(len)
 
print(f"\nTotal pathways defined: {len(mitopathways)}")
print(mitopathways[[PATHWAY_COL, "n_genes"]].sort_values("n_genes", ascending=False).head(20))
```

## 

```{python}
pathway_scores = pd.DataFrame(index=log_expr.columns)
 
for _, row in mitopathways.iterrows():
    pathway_name = row[PATHWAY_COL]
    genes = row["gene_list"]
 
    genes_present = [g for g in genes if g in log_expr.index]
    if len(genes_present) < 3:
        continue  # skip pathways with too few genes present to score meaningfully
 
    sub_expr = log_expr.loc[genes_present]
    z = sub_expr.sub(sub_expr.mean(axis=1), axis=0).div(sub_expr.std(axis=1), axis=0)
    pathway_scores[pathway_name] = z.mean(axis=0)
 
print(f"\nComputed sub-scores for {pathway_scores.shape[1]} pathways")
pathway_scores.to_csv("mitocarta_pathway_subscores.csv")
 
```

## 

```{python}
from statsmodels.stats.multitest import multipletests

sample_type = sample_meta["tcga.cgc_sample_sample_type"]
 
results = []
for pathway in pathway_scores.columns:
    tumor_vals = pathway_scores.loc[sample_type == "Primary Tumor", pathway].dropna()
    normal_vals = pathway_scores.loc[sample_type == "Solid Tissue Normal", pathway].dropna()
 
    if len(tumor_vals) < 5 or len(normal_vals) < 5:
        continue
 
    stat, pval = mannwhitneyu(tumor_vals, normal_vals)
    direction = "UP in tumor" if tumor_vals.mean() > normal_vals.mean() else "DOWN in tumor"
 
    results.append({
        "pathway": pathway,
        "tumor_mean": tumor_vals.mean(),
        "normal_mean": normal_vals.mean(),
        "direction": direction,
        "pvalue": pval,
    })
 
results_df = pd.DataFrame(results).sort_values("pvalue")
 
from statsmodels.stats.multitest import multipletests
results_df["padj"] = multipletests(results_df["pvalue"], method="fdr_bh")[1]
 
results_df.to_csv("mitocarta_pathway_tumor_vs_normal.csv", index=False)
 
print("\n=== Top 20 most different MitoCarta pathways (tumor vs normal) ===")
print(results_df.head(20).to_string())
 
print("\n=== Specifically: OXPHOS-related and fatty-acid-related pathways ===")
oxphos_fa = results_df[results_df["pathway"].str.contains(
    "OXPHOS|oxidative|fatty acid|fat oxidation|electron transport", case=False, na=False
)]
print(oxphos_fa.to_string())
```

Mitochondrial gene expression in breast tumors doesn't move uniformly. Genes involved in mitochondrial biogenesis and protein import (ribosome, chaperones, import channels) are modestly upregulated, consistent with increased mitochondrial mass needed to support rapid proliferation. In contrast, fatty acid oxidation shows the strongest and most consistent downregulation among all MitoCarta pathways tested, the clearest single piece of evidence for metabolic rewiring away from oxidative fuel use. OXPHOS itself shows a small but statistically significant increase using MitoCarta's gene definitions, differing from the Hallmark GSEA result. A reminder that pathway direction can depend on which curated gene set is used, and that "OXPHOS" is not monolithic.

##
